danger_signals.visual_form: Metal cage walls surrounding the wrestling ring
danger_signals.meaning: Match danger escalation — escape/containment threat; wrestler cannot flee arena
Immediate physical threat requiring defensive/evasive action

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite state degradation (knockdown, stagger animations)
danger_signals.meaning: Wrestler approaching defeat state; match outcome in jeopardy

safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone offering tactical recovery; launch point for offensive moves

safety_signals.visual_form: Partner character visible at ring apron in Tag Team modes
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical escape available via tag; can rotate fresh wrestler into match
Safe state; no active threat until match begins

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opponent wrestlers differentiated by character model/sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: WWF-branded ring, arena setting, championship title visuals

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character-specific attire, wrestling personas (e.g., Hulk Hogan, The Undertaker, Papa Shango)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: NES roster: Hulk Hogan, Randy Savage, Ted DiBiase, I.R.S., Bret Hart, The Undertaker, Jake Roberts, Sid Justice, Roddy Piper, The Mountie. Sega roster swaps characters (Ric Flair, Papa Shango, Shawn Michaels, Tatanka replaces some)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Wrestler-specific character sprites, entrance presentation in match modes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two wrestlers in ring; visible health/stamina indicators; opponent proximity and animation states
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Wrestlers perform attacks, grapples, running moves, ground attacks, turnbuckle moves; reactive knockdowns and stagger states

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Metal cage walls enclose ring; wrestler position relative to cage exit/top
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Wrestler must escape cage via door or climb over cage top; cage walls block standard ring exit

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two wrestlers per team; partner visible at apron; active wrestler highlighted/centered in play area
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Active wrestler can tag out to partner; fresh wrestler enters ring; fatigued wrestler retreats to apron

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Championship tournament (WWF Championship / Tag Team Championship)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bracket progression visual; championship title displayed; opponent roster presented sequentially
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Wrestler advances through ladder of opponents; defeated wrestlers removed from roster; victory chain leads to championship crown

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Knockdown animation; character sprite in prone state; match end screen or transition
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Wrestler unable to recover; match concludes; opponent declared victor

environmental_storytelling.example: Championship mode requires defeating all other wrestlers in sequence, visually establishing a ranked competitive order within WWF
Cage match mode represents heightened danger/desperation compared to standard One-on-One match; cage walls are physical manifestation of 'no escape' stakes
Unique wrestler models (Hulk Hogan vs. The Undertaker vs. Papa Shango) communicate distinct personas and real-world wrestling personalities to player; visual differentiation replaces stat variation

environmental_storytelling.technique: Match mode menu as narrative branching
environmental_storytelling.example: Multiple game modes (One-on-One, Tag Team, Championship, Tag Championship) represent different competitive narratives; player selects which 'story' to pursue

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard sprite; responsive to player input; can perform full moveset (punches, kicks, grapples, running attacks, ground attacks, turnbuckle moves)
Staggered or knockdown animation; slower response; reduced movement speed; vulnerability to opponent attacks

entity_behavioral_tells.state: In cage match — near cage top
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Climbing animation; escape attempt behavior; opponent interference from below
Stationary at ring apron; inactive sprite; cannot participate in ring action until tagged in
Prone/knockout animation; disappears from ring; replaced by next opponent in tournament modes
Can execute turnbuckle-specific attack move (implied move off turnbuckle); gain tactical positioning advantage

notes: WWF Steel Cage Challenge is a 1992 NES wrestling game with limited visual complexity. It communicates world-state primarily through: (1) match mode selection (One-on-One, Tag Team, Championship progression); (2) cage presence as danger escalation; (3) character sprite differentiation for wrestler identity; (4) health/stamina depletion animations. The game lacks finishing moves and character-specific movesets, so all wrestlers share identical attack patterns. Visual communication relies on sprite animation, ring positioning, and cage presence rather than color-coded factions or symbolic markers. The steel cage mechanic is the primary world-state escalation signal. Roster variations between NES and Sega versions suggest platform-specific licensing deals but do not affect in-game visual communication mechanics.
sources_used: wikipedia: WWF WrestleMania: Steel Cage Challenge
